plaintext: Humans are soft, emotional, easily overwhelmed animals. But instead of claws or fangs, we defend ourselves with behavioral tools -- spreadsheets and to-do lists that give us a sense of control when we feel uncertain. Sarcasm to deflect vulnerability. A haircut or cool jacket to manage insecurity. The margins before a Mercury flip invite a conscious look at what we use to feel safe and powerful. ARIES (March 21-April 19). Remember when you paid special attention to someone’s preferences and studied their interests like a thesis? Not now. Now you’ll smile and file information away. Cool, composed and still tuned in, your restraint reads as confident charm. Keep Watching Always Early? Here’s What It Reveals About Your Personality, According to PsychologyAlways Early? Here’s What It Reveals About Your Personality, According to Psychology TAURUS (April 20-May 20). You will know the fire of discernment -- the real, sharp-edged awareness that you don’t want to float around the ideas. You want to do something with them. You want action, traction, utility. GEMINI (May 21-June 21). People state their opinions as if they are truth, or latch onto only the part of the story that reflects their view. Even the thing that’s not universally true can still be partly true, and in today’s case this will be a source of provocation. CANCER (June 22-July 22). What does it take to create a better system? How can you turn transactions into a smoother process? The answers you seek will start in awareness. A detail will catch your attention and unlock a bigger understanding. LEO (July 23-Aug. 22). It will be deeply satisfying to put your hands to work today. The result is something to point to and be proud of. But even better -- the process feels right. You’ll fix, build and feel your actions land purposefully in the flow of life. VIRGO (Aug. 23-Sept. 22). This is the time to tweak part of your routine because the new way will stick. Daily rituals can be art. Then the joy won’t just be in the outcome. It’s in the grace of doing it well, again and again -- the elegance in repetition. LIBRA (Sept. 23-Oct. 23). The lessons are complex. People learn at different rates and times, or perhaps not at all, and that’s OK, too. Your compassion is needed once more. We are all babies in some way. When people know better, they do better. SCORPIO (Oct. 24-Nov. 21). Your curiosity drops into places others wouldn’t think to investigate. They don’t see the issue. Perhaps it’s too ordinary to detect. But you know that boring problems often have interesting solutions, and you’ll find them. SAGITTARIUS (Nov. 22-Dec. 21). You can’t keep your own secrets today. Your opinion affects things even if you don’t say it out loud. Your vibes tell what’s going on inside you. The confidence, sadness, anger, love, competition or joy you feel will make change. CAPRICORN (Dec. 22-Jan. 19). Social responsibility is the theme. People may be quietly struggling. Not everyone asks for what they need. Staying connected and checking in is real work -- not a distraction from life, but the stuff of life. AQUARIUS (Jan. 20-Feb. 18). A stellar presentation turns heads, stirs appetites, gets attention. Everything acceptable, expected, respectable, pretty good is in that mid-zone, which gets very little notice. Eyes go to the terrible and the exceptional. PISCES (Feb. 19-March 20). You’ve an excellent read of the room today. Whether the room is homogenous, diverse, chaotic or catatonic, you’ll sense it, read it and respond to it. And with your bright energy, you’ll improve it. TODAY’S BIRTHDAY (Aug. 10). Welcome to your year of truth. You will bring the light of honesty to relationships, add the glow of realism to superficial environments and even your rants and reckonings will reward, as wrongs get righted. More highlights: a prize is fulfilling because of how hard and smart you worked for it. Interpersonal skills have you shining in multiple environments, which is fortuitous since you’ll travel at least three times. Gemini and Aquarius adore you. Your lucky numbers are: 7, 2, 20, 14 and 1. The United States has announced a pause on all routine visa applications for citizens of Zimbabwe. The State Department said in a statement on Thursday that the US embassy in Zimbabwe would pause all routine visa services starting from Friday “while we address concerns with the Government of Zimbabwe”. The embassy described the measure as temporary and part of the Trump administration’s efforts to “prevent visa overstay and misuse”. Most diplomatic and official visas would be exempt from the pause, the US said. The US has enforced new travel restrictions on citizens from several African countries under President Donald Trump’s broader immigration enforcement policies. In June, the US put in place travel bans on citizens from 12 countries, seven of them in Africa. It increased restrictions on seven other nations, three of them African. The US has also demanded that 36 countries, the majority of them in Africa, improve their vetting of travellers or face a ban on their citizens visiting the United States. Zimbabwe, Malawi and Zambia were all on that list of 36 countries asked to improve their citizens’ travel documentation and take steps to address the status of their nationals who are in the US illegally. Under the programme, citizens of those countries must also arrive and depart at one of three airports: Boston’s Logan International Airport, New York’s John F Kennedy International Airport or Dulles International Airport near Washington, DC. The visa bond pilot programme will start on August 20, the State Department said. Press Release Press Release · Tuesday, October 21, 1997 Press Release October 21, 1997 Designs for Democracy: 200 Years of Drawings from the National Archives Washington, DC. . . Over the course of its history, the United States government has prepared, commissioned, received or approved designs for millions of objects. These objects range in size from minuscule to mammoth, from chandelier lamps to expansion bridges. The design for each of these objects was created in response to a specific Federal policy or program and reflects the diversity and creativity that forms the core of the American character. Nearly 100 of these drawings will be on display in "Designs for Democracy," a major exhibition in the Circular Gallery of the National Archives Building that opens to the public on March 13, 1998. The exhibition, which is free, will remain open through January 10, 1999. The National Archives and Records Administration is located on Constitution Avenue, between 7th and 9th Streets, NW. These designs were originally created to support military objectives, provide civilian services and facilitate the conduct of government business. As a result, drawings can be found among NARA’s records of a great many Federal agencies. "Designs for Democracy" highlights some of the essential evidence representing 200 years of Federal design. "Designs for Democracy" includes elegant watercolors, exquisite ink and wash drawings, bold charcoal and pencil sketches and finely executed engineering details that were created by professional artists, engineers, inventors, draftsmen or graphic artists -- a few were submitted by inspired citizens. While some bear the signature of a well-known designer, many the imprimatur of an approving Federal official, often they are unsigned and their creator remains unknown. Highlights In the exhibition include: Sketch of the Great Seal By Francis Hopkinson, May 10, 1780 Pencil and ink on paper Records of the Department of State Although Francis Hopkinson’s 1780 design for the Great Seal of the United States was not accepted, several of its components were incorporated into the approved design of 1782. Hopkinson introduced the arrow and olive branch as symbols of war and peace. He also suggested the red, white and blue colors for the shield. "Plans for the Arlington Memorial Bridge"
William Mitchell Kendall, architect, McKim, Mead, and White, for the Arlington Memorial Bridge Commission, 1923
Pencil, ink and wash on paper Records of the Office of Public Buildings and Public Parks of the National Capital
In 1922 the Memorial Bridge Commission selected William Mitchell Kendall’s design for a bridge which would link Washington, D.C., to Virginia’s Arlington Cemetery. Kendall’s design served not only as a memorial to Americans who had died in military service, but also as the symbolic reunification of North and South after the Civil War. This rendering measures 3 ½ feet by 11 feet, showing the bridge from two perspectives: one from the south side of the Lincoln Memorial looking across to the Custis-Lee Mansion; the other from mid-Potomac River. Submarine Torpedo Boat
By John P. Holland, February 18, 1875
Ink on paper Records of the Office of the Chief of Ordnance
In 1875 Irish American John H. Holland sent the U.S. Navy this design for an experimental 15 ½-foot long torpedo boat, designed to operate underwater. Although this design was rejected, Holland continued to improve his invention and on October 12, 1900, the U.S. Navy commissioned the first true submarine, the 64-foot U.S.S. Holland Walt Disney Design for 114 Signal Intelligence Company
By an unknown artist, 1942
Gouache on card stock Records of the Adjutant General’s Office, 1917 -
During World War II, the Walt Disney Company assigned artists to design approximately 1,200 unit insignias for the armed forces. Disney gave unrestricted use of the insignias to the armed forces, but stipulated that any profit-making enterprises needed to be approved by the company. Other entertainment companies, notably Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer and Warner Brothers, also donated insignia designs. This design is reminiscent of one of the characters in the 1934 cartoon The Grasshopper and the Ants. "John Gorham’s Design for Spoons"
By John Gorham
Patent granted April 10, 1855
Watercolor and wash on paper Records of the Patent and Trademark Office
John Gorham applied for a design patent for his pattern of decoration for a spoon. This type of patent is issued for artistic ornamentation of manufactured objects. The Biosystems Technology & Analytics (BTA) curriculum is intended to prepare students for careers in the 4th Industrial Revolution which is the intersection of science, technology, and analytics. Meeting the grand challenges of tomorrow will require a convergence of new ideas, approaches, and technologies from diverse disciplines. In addition to general education requirements, our students will obtain foundational training in the tools needed to solve the grand challenges of the future: science (3 years of science courses: 1 year biology, 1 year chemistry, half-year physics, and half-year of either microbiology, biochemistry, or genetics), technology (1 semester of sensors and controls, 1 semester of remote sensing, 2 semesters of computer skills), and 5 courses of data science and analytics (1 semester of data science, 1 semester of statistics, 1 semester of bioinformatics, 2 semesters of analytics). Students in this program will be able to use those tools to engage technology and data challenges in precision agriculture, remote sensing, water management, statistical informatics, data-mining, and cyberinfrastructure development. Given massive unstructured "big data" streaming from sensors, controls, cloud repositories, and the interactive web, data-driven discovery is now possible.
Still, converting this knowledge into action to solve real-world problems in the life sciences requires a merger of data science, biology and physics, and applied engineering (also known as the 4th Industrial Revolution). These efforts require innovative and integrated educational pathways in applied statistics, computer science, and engineering technology. The Biosystems Engineering department is well-positioned to bring together these interdisciplinary skills into a solid program in the life sciences and this emerging program in Biosystems Technology & Analytics: 4 faculty in water resources, 6 faculty in the analytics of biological data, 5 faculty in modeling and sensors for agriculture and bioreactors, and 4 faculty in field-level precision agriculture (note that some of the faculty have more than one area of expertise). The goal of this proposed program is to create an inspirational and transformative training experience that integrates intellectual merit with real world, hands-on experiences across disciplines (genomics, precision agriculture, data science, and engineering technology); scales (molecules to models); and trainees (from undergraduate students to graduate students).
With the backdrop of the new information age, these emerging areas of engineering technology and applied data science warrant the need for a new program in Biosystems Technology & Analytics. This program will train students to take a true systems-level approach to understanding the relationships across food, water, and energy for a sustainable future by providing skills in sensors and remote sensing (data acquisition), statistics and bioinformatics (data management and analytics), and applying these tools to solve real-world problems. Learning Outcomes Demonstrate the ability to assess and solve problems. Investigate, explain, critique, and integrate principles from math and sciences such as biology, microbiology, metagenomics, chemistry, statistics, computing, and programming for application in sustainable biological systems. Conduct experiments and analyze and interpret data. Apply quantitative principles to solve problems. Demonstrate the ability to work in teams integrating science and technology to solve problems involving sustainable biological systems. Demonstrate effective communication skills, both written and verbal. Demonstrate an understanding of professional and ethical responsibility. This strand involves skill and facility with calculus. The S-strand begins at one of two calculus options - MATH 122A/B (Functions for Calculus and First-Semester Calculus) or MATH 125 (Calculus I). Students who successfully complete the S-strand may continue on to MATH 129 (Calculus II), MATH 223 (Vector Calculus), or beyond. Those who choose but are not ready to begin the S-strand will be required to take preparatory work. Second-semester skill level in a second language is required.
Non-B.A. degree students may fulfill this requirement with one of the following options:
1. Scoring the equivalent of second-semester skill level on an entrance or placement examination administered by The University of Arizona.
2. Completion with a C or better of a second semester course at the post-secondary level.
3. An AP (Advanced Placement), IB (International Baccalaureate) or CLEP (College Level Entrance Program) language score determined by the individual language department.
4. In the College of Engineering by a method determined within the College; however, all students in the college must also take a language placement examination on entrance to the University. Credit: Grant Webster | Food Styling: Emily Nienhaus If our collective obsession with the chocolate muffins at the 2024 Olympics and an entire Seinfeld episode devoted to “The Muffin Top” weren’t proof enough that Americans are sweet on muffins, perhaps this will seal the deal. Several times since “William Shatner’s” Cappuccino Muffins first graced the internet, they’ve had sparks of viral success on blogs and social media. I put the Star Trek actor’s name in quotes because the jury is still out if this is actually William Shatner's Cappuccino Muffin recipe. It’s been credited to him since at least 2007, when Anna Ginsberg of Cookie Madness originally shared her rendition. The buzzy morning muffins have been making the rounds online ever since—still attributed to Shatner. Ginsberg credits Recipe Goldmine as the place she first discovered the formula, and I was unable to find more about the history of this exact baked good. Still, Shatner has been embracing the “live long and prosper” philosophy for 94 years, so he’s bound to have made his fair share of recipes in his day…so maybe this is one of them. With that in mind, I decided to go boldly into the kitchen to find out how these muffins stack up. Did you know you can save all of your favorite Better Homes & Gardens recipes in one spot? MyRecipes is the digital recipe box that helps you keep all the recipes you love within reach. Regardless of who first dreamed up this Cappuccino Muffin recipe, it’s easy to understand the appeal. The instructions ask for just 11 ingredients total, and the average basic kitchen is stocked with 10—if not all 11—of those items as we speak. Plus, as you might guess based on the name, these muffins are designed to combine the best parts of a breakfast pastry and a cup of Joe in each bite. Here’s how to make them: Combine the dry ingredients. Into a large bowl, sift all-purpose flour, sugar, baking powder, instant espresso powder, cinnamon, and salt. Prepare the wet ingredients. In another medium bowl, beat an egg with milk and melted butter. Add a splash of vanilla extract and stir to combine. Mix the batter. Make a well in the center of the flour, then pour in the milk mixture. Pour in chocolate chips. Using a spatula, stir until the flour is just moistened and mostly incorporated (a few small clumps are okay). Scoop and bake. Grease muffin tins or line them with paper baking cups, then scoop batter into each well, filling each about two-thirds full. Bake at 400°F for 20 minutes. Although it’s tempting to reach for a boxed mix (and I have no shame in doing so!), this Cappuccino Muffin recipe was a lovely reminder that a batch of homemade muffins can be on the table in just half an hour. If you’re feeling inspired to give “Shatner’s” strategy a try too, here are a few helpful hints from our Test Kitchen and my experiment with this and other similar muffin recipes: Try a chopped chocolate bar. Chocolate chips are formulated to maintain their cute kiss shape, which means they tend to resist melting. Compared to chocolate bars, chips usually have less cocoa butter content and more stabilizers. If you prefer pockets of melted chocolate, take a cue from our Chocolate-Cherry Muffin recipe and chop up a chocolate bar (with your desired sweetness level). Fold in ¾ cup chocolate pieces instead of using chips. Spike your batter. With just 2 teaspoons of instant espresso powder in the 12-muffin recipe, I found the coffee flavor to be quite mild. I’m not a big coffee fan, so faint java flavor is just fine with me. However, if you crave more kick, follow the lead of our five-star Vietnamese Coffee Cake Muffins and replace ¼ cup of the milk in the wet mixture with cooled brewed espresso. Stagger the muffins. If you have some breathing room in your baking time, or if your baking toolkit includes a couple muffin tins, I like to space out the muffins by skipping a well between each that I fill. This ends up looking like a 2-1-2-1 pattern. (Fill the empty cups with a splash of water so you don’t scorch your pan.) Although this muffin hack requires more time or multiple pans, I find that it’s well worth it to promote better air circulation—which leads to more even baking. The muffin tops tend to dome up better, too. Test for doneness. As written, this muffin recipe simply says “bake until just done, approximately 20 minutes.” This begs the question, “what does ‘done’ mean?” Our Test Kitchen swears by the toothpick test for muffins, quick breads, and cakes. It’s easy: Simply insert a wooden toothpick near the center of a baked good. If you remove the toothpick and it appears clean (without wet batter or big clumps stuck to it), the recipe is done. Opt for a thin, sharp knife or a bamboo skewer if you don’t have a toothpick. If you’d like some extra insurance that you haven’t underbaked, gently press on the top of the muffin. If it springs back, it should be good to go. If the indentation remains, bake for a few minutes more. Store with care. It’s tough to beat a still-warm-from-the-oven muffin, and they’re often best enjoyed the day they are baked. But if you happen to have extra after making muffins, transfer them to an airtight container and keep at room temperature for up to 4 days. Or if you’d like more time—and a gift for your future self—pop cooled muffins into a zip-top bag, label with the name and date, and freeze for up to 3 months. Thaw overnight at room temperature before enjoying. The Verdict on William Shatner's Cappuccino Muffins Often, starting my day with a sweet baked good has me anxious about the impending sugar rush…and crash. But I found these muffins to be soft, lofty, tender, and just sweet enough. Even as a non-coffee drinker, I enjoyed starting my day with a warm Cappuccino Muffin, a carton of Greek yogurt, and fruit salad. I found that breakfast line-up to keep my energy even-keeled for several hours. If you prefer your muffins on the sweater side, try drizzling a vanilla, espresso, or chocolate glaze on top. Coffee fans might crave more flavor, so I’d recommend the espresso-for-milk swap mentioned above. As for me, the only thing missing was a bit more cinnamon. That’s one spice I measure with my heart, rather than by the spoonful, so ½ teaspoon felt a bit timid for my taste. But that’s one of the terrific things about spices; they’re generally quite flexible and adding a little more or less likely won’t throw off the chemistry involved in a recipe. Yes, even a baked one (designed by Captain Kirk or otherwise)!

Whether you seek historic cities, scenic landscapes, fun places to go or all of the above, Spain is a legendary vacation spot. And for many, visiting Spain is a bucket-list destination, where layers of history and modern life are intertwined at every stage. Enjoy world-class art, classical guitar music, Spanish cuisine and interesting things to do, but don’t forget to explore beyond the ordinary on your trip to Spain! When you travel to Spain, a vast and vibrant country in Southern Europe, let AAA’s travel experts help you with designing itineraries, booking accommodations and tours, and sharing travel tips to ensure a seamless experience. Many people think of Barcelona and Madrid as the best places to visit in Spain, but feel free to broaden your horizons, add some off-the-beaten-path adventures and see some of the most beautiful places in Spain with less-visited attractions. Enhance your Spain vacation with one of two great Spanish driving routes, the Vía de la Plata or The Grand Tour of Catalonia, both of which take you to some of the best sites in Spain. Get exclusive AAA member benefits when renting a car and enjoy the best scenic drives in Spain, such as the Vía de la Plata and Grand Tour of Catalonia. Book tours and things to do in Spain using Trip Canvas. A road trip allows you to fully savor regional restaurants, wineries and diverse scenic landscapes as well as get to know the people and places of Spain. Proudly in cooperation with ROADTRIP Ruta Vía de la Plata This historic yet contemporary road trip from Seville to Gijón in northern Spain, the Ruta Vía de la Plata, features a number of heritage communities and landmarks. Following an old Roman route, the south to north Spain road trip meanders through four spectacular regions: Andalusia, Extremadura, Castilla y León and Asturias. Set off on an adventurous 7-day road trip traveling in Spain by car, or take a leisurely journey in stages by bike or by foot, discovering a versatile route to Spain’s less crowded attractions. Start in Seville, which was once Roman Hispalis, to embrace flamenco, classical guitar and other cultural attractions. Cities Cáceres, Mérida and León offer captivating architectural and scenic splendor as you go. Zamora is one of the Romanesque capitals, where you can see pre-Romanesque Asturian sites, such as the UNESCO World Heritage Site Church of Santa Cristina de Lena, on the way to port town of Gijón, known for its beautiful beaches. Along the way, you’ll enjoy the best food and wine while immersing yourself in the sights and sounds of Spain. Introducing our Southwestern Chicken Quesadilla recipe that will treat your taste buds to bold and exciting flavors. This dish takes the best elements of Southwestern cuisine and combines them into a convenient and delicious meal. Start by mixing together your grilled chicken, black beans, whole-kernel corn and fresh organic baby spinach in a large bowl. To this mixture, add a generous portion of shredded Mexican cheese, ensuring each bite captures a harmonious blend of these vibrant flavors. With your filling ready, it's time to craft the quesadillas. Warm up a large sauté pan over medium-high heat. After a light mist of cooking spray, place a tortilla onto the pan's surface. Spoon about 1/8th of your vibrant chicken mixture onto half the tortilla, then fold it over, encapsulating the rich blend of ingredients. Allow each side to achieve a golden hue, taking about a minute per side. Once done, let it rest briefly, letting the warmth meld the flavors together. Repeat this process with the remaining tortillas. The end result is a platter of quesadillas, each filled with a rich combination of ingredients that showcase the depth and diversity of Southwestern cuisine. They're great for sharing with friends and family, making any mealtime a special occasion. In a large bowl, combine chicken, black beans, corn, spinach and cheese. Reserve. Heat a large sauté pan over medium-high heat. Lightly spray with cooking spray, add one tortilla to the pan. Place a generous portion of chicken mixture on only half of the tortilla, fold the other half on top. Cook for one minute, turn and cook for an additional minute or until golden brown. Repeat for remaining 7 tortillas.

With the bookplate of Scofield Thayer [1889-1982] -- co-owner and editor of the Dial Magazine during its most important period in the 1920's. Thayer was a Milton Academy, and later Harvard, with T. S. Eliot. While at Harvard, Thayer worked on the staff of the 'Harvard Monthly.' At college, he came to know E. E. Cummings, Alan Seeger, Lincoln MacVeagh, Arthur Wilson (later known as Winslow Wilson) and Gilbert Seldes, and James Sibley Watson Jr. Both Thayer and Watson were wealthy heirs; together they purchased 'The Dial.' "Their" first issue was January, 1920, and before the magazine ceased publication in 1929, it became a key repository of modernism in literature and art. An issue from 1922, much sought by collectors, contains the initial appearance of T. S. Eliot's 'The Wasteland.' Even after he sailed off to Europe in the summer of 1921, Thayer continued to provide editorial work and advice for the magazine. He settled in Vienna, where he became a patient of Dr. Sigmund Freud. This fact suggests both that Thayer's wealth allowed him to pay for the very best, and that his German was sufficiently fluent to conduct an analysis in that language. By the middle of the 1920's Thayer admitted to himself and associates that he was undergoing a serious mental breakdown. He pulled back from the 'Dial' and spent the rest of a long life travelling between residences in Bermuda, Florida, Boston, and his family's compound on Martha's Vineyard. His art collection was left to the Metropolitan Museum, his collection of Aubrey Beardsley drawings is now at Harvard's Fogg Museum. Yale has his papers, (including some Dial archives) -- a collection of about 100 boxes, most of which spent many decades in the Worcester Storage Company. Yale notes that there is no evidence that Thayer answered any letters from any of his many friends received after February of 1926. A biography of his unusual life was published eight years ago ['The Tortured Life of Scofield Thayer' by James Dempsey]. His charming bookplate with a striking image of a pomegranate was designed in 1913 by the artist Sydney Lawton Smith [1845-1929 -- thanks are in order to the Boston Athenaeum, whose webpage confirm the identity of the artist who designed Thayer's bookplate ]. The plate includes the motto: "Sub cortice dulcedo" -- under the bark is sweetness. An appropriate motto for a bookplate, if one considers the "bark" to be emblematic of the covers of a book, and "sweetness" a characterization of the book's contents. It is easy to see why this little gem of Jugendstil design, elegantly containing one of Theodor Storm's best novellas in his late realistic manner might have appealed to Scofield Thayer. Certainly, he could instead have bought an inexpensive edition of this text from Insel Verlag as a new book during his time residing in Vienna. Indeed, it is possible, or even likely, that Thayer acquired this 1905 edition during his earlier school days as a new book; accompanied by his tutor, he traveled extensively in France, Germany, Switzerland, and Italy during the summer before he entered Harvard College in 1909. He continued spending summers in Germany and Italy during his years at Harvard. At Magdalen, Oxford in 1913 he renewed his relationship with old schoolmate T. S. Eliot -- in fact, Thayer is reported to have introduced Eliot to Vivien Heigh-Wood who would become the poet's first wife. The Names of God Bible (NOG) accurately translates the meaning of the original texts into clear, everyday language. While most translations obscure the names and titles of God by replacing them with just a few English words such as God, Lord, or LORD, The Names of God Bible restores the transliterations of ancient names—such as Yahweh, El Shadday, El Elyon, and Adonay—to help the reader better understand the rich meaning of God’s names that are found in the original Hebrew and Aramaic text. One of the best ways to get to know God on a deeper level is to know his names and titles as they are revealed in Scripture. If you are interested in more information about the meaning of the names of God, the print edition of The Names of God Bible includes commentary by bestselling author Ann Spangler, sharing background information, key Scripture passages, devotional readings, and Bible promises connected to each name. Show more questions More about Guinea Discover the Hidden Gems of Guinea Guinea is a land of extraordinary beauty and rich cultural heritage, often overshadowed by its more popular neighbors. This West African country offers a plethora of hidden gems that beckon adventurous travelers. From the breathtaking landscapes of the Fouta Djallon highlands to the serene beaches along the Atlantic coast, Guinea has something for everyone. The lush green hills, cascading waterfalls, and vibrant wildlife create an enchanting backdrop for exploration. One of the lesser-known attractions is the stunning Mount Nimba, a UNESCO World Heritage site. This majestic mountain range is home to diverse flora and fauna, making it a paradise for nature lovers and hikers. The area is known for its rich biodiversity, including several endemic species that can only be found here. Trekking through the rugged terrain offers not only a physical challenge but also the chance to witness breathtaking views and unique wildlife up close. In addition to natural wonders, Guinea boasts a vibrant local culture that is waiting to be discovered. The bustling markets, traditional music, and colorful festivals provide an authentic experience that immerses visitors in the local way of life. The city of Conakry, the capital, is a great starting point for exploring the country’s cultural richness. Here, travelers can visit the Guinea National Museum to learn about the country’s history and heritage, or explore the lively streets filled with artisans showcasing their crafts. Unforgettable Experiences Await in Guinea Traveling to Guinea promises a wealth of unforgettable experiences that will leave a lasting impression. One of the top reasons to visit is the country’s rich cultural heritage. Guinea is home to over 24 ethnic groups, each with its own unique traditions, languages, and customs. Engaging with local communities offers insights into their way of life, and visitors often find themselves welcomed with open arms. Participating in traditional ceremonies or festivals allows travelers to experience the vibrant culture firsthand, making for truly memorable moments. Wildlife enthusiasts will be thrilled by the diverse array of species that inhabit Guinea’s national parks and reserves. The Upper Niger National Park is a must-visit for those looking to spot rare birds and other wildlife in their natural habitat. The park is known for its rich biodiversity, including elephants, hippos, and various primate species. Whether you’re a seasoned birdwatcher or simply enjoy the beauty of nature, the sights and sounds of Guinea’s wildlife will captivate your senses. For those seeking adventure, the breathtaking natural scenery offers endless opportunities for exploration. The stunning waterfalls at Kambadaga and the picturesque landscapes of the Fouta Djallon region provide perfect settings for hiking, picnicking, or simply soaking in the beauty of nature. The combination of lush greenery, dramatic cliffs, and cascading waters creates a serene environment that invites relaxation and adventure alike. When to Plan Your Adventure in Guinea Timing is key when planning your visit to Guinea to ensure an enjoyable experience. The best time to explore this beautiful country is during the dry season, which typically runs from November to April. During these months, the weather is pleasantly warm, and rainfall is minimal, making it ideal for outdoor activities and sightseeing. The dry season also coincides with various cultural festivals, providing visitors with the chance to witness vibrant local celebrations. In contrast, the rainy season, which lasts from May to October, can bring heavy downpours and humidity. While the landscapes become lush and vibrant during this time, travel may be more challenging due to muddy roads and limited access to certain areas. However, for those who appreciate the beauty of nature in its full bloom, visiting during the rainy season can offer a unique perspective on Guinea's natural wonders. Additionally, keep an eye on local events and festivals that might coincide with your travel dates. Participating in these celebrations can enhance your experience and provide deeper insights into the culture. Whether it's music festivals, traditional ceremonies, or local markets, being part of these events will make your trip to Guinea even more special. Essential Travel Tips for Exploring Guinea Traveling to Guinea can be an enriching experience, but being well-prepared is essential for a smooth journey. Understanding local customs and cultural etiquette is crucial. For example, greetings are very important in Guinean culture. A friendly handshake accompanied by a smile goes a long way. It is also customary to inquire about a person’s well-being and family before diving into conversations, reflecting the value placed on relationships. Safety is another important consideration for travelers. While Guinea is generally safe, it’s wise to stay vigilant, especially in crowded areas. Avoid displaying valuables and be cautious when using public transportation. Having a local guide can enhance your experience and provide valuable insights while ensuring your safety. Additionally, it's advisable to have travel insurance that covers medical emergencies and unexpected cancellations. Learning a few phrases in the local language, French, can also enhance interactions with locals. Simple greetings like “Bonjour” (Hello) and “Merci” (Thank you) can go a long way in building rapport. Many locals appreciate when visitors make an effort to speak their language, no matter how basic. This small gesture can lead to more meaningful connections and experiences during your travels. Where to Lay Your Head in Guinea Finding the right place to stay is essential for any traveler, and Guinea offers a variety of accommodations to suit different preferences and budgets. From luxurious hotels to cozy guesthouses, there’s something for everyone. For those seeking a touch of elegance, the Noom Hotel Conakry stands out with its modern amenities and stunning views of the Atlantic Ocean. Guests rave about the cleanliness of the rooms and the delightful breakfast buffet, making it a popular choice for both business and leisure travelers. The friendly and accommodating staff further enhances the experience, ensuring a comfortable stay. If a more traditional atmosphere is what you’re after, consider the Hotel Azur Conakry. This hotel combines comfort with a warm, welcoming ambiance. It’s conveniently located near various attractions, making it easy to explore the city. Guests appreciate the spacious rooms and the opportunity to enjoy local cuisine at the on-site restaurant, which serves a variety of dishes that reflect the rich culinary heritage of Guinea. For budget-conscious travelers, the Palm Camayenne offers a great balance of affordability and comfort. This hotel provides essential amenities and is well-situated for those looking to experience the local culture. Getting Around in Guinea Made Easy Exploring Guinea can be an exciting adventure, and understanding the transportation options available will enhance your travel experience. In the capital city of Conakry, public transport is a viable option for getting around. Buses and shared taxis, known as “clandos,” are prevalent and provide an affordable way to navigate the city. However, it's important to be aware of the bus schedules and routes, as they may not always be clearly marked. For those who prefer more convenience, hiring a private taxi is an excellent choice. Taxis are readily available and can be flagged down on the street or booked through your hotel. Agreeing on the fare before starting your journey is advisable, as most taxis do not have meters. This way, you can avoid any surprises at the end of your ride. Additionally, many hotels offer shuttle services to popular attractions, which can simplify your travel plans. If you’re feeling adventurous, renting a car can provide the freedom to explore Guinea at your own pace. Several rental companies operate in Conakry, offering a range of vehicles to suit your needs. However, driving conditions can be challenging due to road conditions and traffic, so ensure you are comfortable with local driving regulations before hitting the road. With the right transportation options, navigating Guinea will be a breeze, allowing you to fully immerse yourself in the country's beauty and culture. Thrilling Activities to Experience in Guinea Guinea is a playground for adventure enthusiasts, offering a wide array of activities that cater to different interests. One of the most popular pursuits is hiking in the stunning Fouta Djallon region. This mountainous area is known for its breathtaking landscapes, including lush valleys, dramatic cliffs, and cascading waterfalls. Hikers can choose from various trails that range in difficulty, making it suitable for both seasoned trekkers and casual walkers. The fresh air and picturesque scenery make every step worthwhile, and the chance to encounter local wildlife adds to the excitement. For those seeking a more adrenaline-pumping experience, consider exploring the national parks and reserves. The Upper Niger National Park is an excellent destination for wildlife spotting, where visitors can observe elephants, hippos, and a variety of bird species in their natural habitat. Guided tours are available, providing insights into the park's ecosystem and ensuring that you don’t miss out on any of the incredible sights. Whether you're an avid birdwatcher or simply enjoy the beauty of nature, the park offers an unforgettable experience. Cultural experiences also abound in Guinea. Engaging with local communities through workshops or cultural tours allows travelers to learn about traditional crafts, music, and dance. Participating in these activities not only enriches your understanding of Guinean culture but also creates lasting memories. With so many thrilling activities to choose from, Guinea is sure to satisfy the adventurous spirit in every traveler. Shop Like a Local in Guinea Shopping in Guinea is a delightful experience that allows visitors to immerse themselves in the local culture. Markets are the heart of shopping in Guinea, offering everything from handcrafted goods to fresh produce. The Madina Market in Conakry is a bustling hub where you can find an array of items, including colorful textiles, traditional crafts, and delicious street food. Strolling through the market, you’ll encounter friendly vendors eager to share their stories and products, making it a great place to practice your French and engage with locals. For those interested in unique souvenirs, look for artisanal crafts such as woven baskets, wooden carvings, and traditional jewelry. These items not only make for great gifts but also support local artisans and their communities. Bargaining is common in local markets, so don’t hesitate to negotiate prices while keeping the experience friendly and respectful. In addition to markets, various shops in Conakry offer a selection of local and imported goods. From clothing boutiques to specialty stores, shoppers can find a range of products that reflect the vibrant culture of Guinea. Whether you’re looking for a special keepsake or simply want to explore, shopping in Guinea provides an authentic experience that you won't want to miss. Nature Lovers' Paradise in Guinea Guinea is a dream destination for nature lovers, boasting a stunning array of national parks and nature reserves that showcase the country's diverse ecosystems. One of the crown jewels is the Upper Niger National Park, which spans over 1,800 square kilometers. This park is home to a rich variety of wildlife, including elephants, hippos, and numerous bird species, making it a fantastic spot for birdwatchers and wildlife enthusiasts. The park's rivers and wetlands provide a unique habitat, and guided tours often reveal the secrets of its flourishing ecosystem. Visitors can embark on boat rides to explore the waterways, offering a different perspective on the park's natural beauty. Another must-visit location is the Mount Nimba Strict Nature Reserve, recognized as a UNESCO World Heritage site. The reserve features breathtaking mountain landscapes, lush forests, and rare plant species that thrive in its unique climate. Hiking trails lead adventurous travelers through this remarkable terrain, offering opportunities to encounter endemic species and enjoy panoramic views from the summits. The area is not just a haven for flora and fauna but also a cultural treasure, as local communities often engage in sustainable practices that protect their environment while preserving their traditions. For those who prefer a more relaxed experience, the scenic beaches along the Atlantic coast provide a perfect escape. The coastline is dotted with charming fishing villages where visitors can unwind, enjoy fresh seafood, and soak in the sun. Beaches like Rond Point and Kassa Island offer tranquil settings for swimming, picnicking, or simply basking in the beauty of the ocean. Whether trekking through national parks or lounging by the beach, nature lovers will find endless opportunities to connect with Guinea's breathtaking landscapes. Unearthing the Rich History of Guinea Guinea's rich history is reflected in its numerous historical and cultural sites, each telling a story of the country's past and its people. The Guinea National Museum in Conakry is a great starting point for history buffs. The museum houses a vast collection of artifacts, including traditional masks, musical instruments, and textiles that showcase the diverse cultures of Guinea's 24 ethnic groups. Visitors can gain insights into the country's heritage, from ancient civilizations to contemporary traditions, making it a fascinating stop on any itinerary. Another significant site is the Conakry Grand Mosque, which stands as a testament to Guinea's Islamic heritage. This architectural marvel is not only a place of worship but also a symbol of the community's faith and resilience. Visitors are often struck by the mosque's intricate designs and serene atmosphere, making it a peaceful spot for reflection. Guided tours are available, providing a deeper understanding of the mosque's history and its role in the lives of local residents. The Monument de la Revolution is another important landmark that commemorates Guinea's independence and the struggles of its people. The monument, with its towering structure and symbolic sculptures, serves as a reminder of the nation’s journey towards freedom. Exploring these historical sites allows travelers to appreciate the rich tapestry of Guinea's past while connecting with the culture and spirit of its people. Family-Friendly Adventures in Guinea Guinea offers a variety of family-friendly activities that cater to visitors of all ages. For families looking to immerse themselves in nature, the Conakry Botanical Garden is an excellent choice. This expansive garden features a diverse collection of plants, trees, and flowers, providing a beautiful setting for a leisurely stroll. Children can enjoy exploring the gardens, while parents can relax in the serene environment. Educational programs and guided tours are often available, making it a fun and informative outing for the whole family. Another exciting option is a visit to the National Park of Badiar, which offers a chance to see wildlife in their natural habitat. Families can embark on guided safaris, where knowledgeable guides share insights about the local fauna and flora. Spotting animals like monkeys, antelopes, and various bird species can be a thrilling experience for kids and adults alike. The park's picnic areas provide a perfect spot for families to enjoy a packed lunch amidst nature. For a taste of local culture, participating in community workshops can be a fantastic experience for families. Many local artisans offer classes in traditional crafts, such as weaving or pottery, allowing children to engage in hands-on learning while creating their own souvenirs. These workshops not only foster creativity but also encourage family bonding through shared experiences. With so many family-friendly adventures available, Guinea ensures that every member of the family has a memorable trip. Festivals and Events That Celebrate Guinea Guinea's vibrant culture comes alive through its festivals and events, offering travelers a unique opportunity to engage with the local community. One of the most celebrated events is the Fête de la Musique, which takes place in June and showcases the country’s rich musical heritage. Musicians from various genres, including traditional and contemporary, take to the streets to perform, creating an electrifying atmosphere filled with rhythm and joy. This festival is a great way to experience the diverse sounds of Guinea while mingling with locals who share a passion for music. The Guinea National Festival of Arts and Culture is another highlight, celebrating the country’s artistic talents through dance, theater, and visual arts. Held annually, this festival features performances from local artists and groups, providing a platform for cultural exchange. Visitors can witness traditional dances, storytelling, and art exhibitions, making it an enriching experience that highlights Guinea's creative spirit. For those visiting during the harvest season, the Fête de la Moisson offers a glimpse into the agricultural traditions of Guinea. This festival is marked by colorful parades, traditional games, and feasting, celebrating the hard work of farmers and the abundance of crops. Engaging in these local festivities not only enhances the travel experience but also fosters connections with the community, making for unforgettable memories. Transportation Details for a Smooth Journey Getting around Guinea can be an adventure in itself, with various transportation options available to travelers. In Conakry, the capital city, public transportation is both affordable and accessible. Buses and shared taxis, known as “clandos,” are commonly used by locals and provide a great way to experience the city. However, it’s essential to familiarize yourself with the routes and schedules, as they may not always be clearly marked. Riding the clandos can be a fun way to mingle with locals while exploring the city. For more convenience, private taxis are widely available and can be easily hailed on the street or booked through hotels. It’s advisable to agree on the fare before starting your journey, as most taxis do not operate on a meter. This practice helps avoid misunderstandings and ensures a smoother travel experience. Many hotels also offer shuttle services to popular attractions, which can be a hassle-free option for visitors. For those who prefer more freedom, renting a car is an option, though it’s important to be cautious of local driving conditions, which can be challenging. Several rental companies operate in Conakry, offering a range of vehicles. Ensure you have a valid driver’s license and are familiar with local traffic rules before hitting the road. With the right transportation choices, exploring Guinea can be both enjoyable and rewarding. Practical Travel Information for a Hassle-Free Visit When planning a trip to Guinea, being informed about practical travel details is essential for a smooth experience. Visa requirements vary depending on your nationality, so it’s important to check the latest regulations before traveling. Most visitors will need to obtain a visa prior to arrival, which can be done through Guinean embassies or consulates. Ensure your passport is valid for at least six months beyond your planned departure date, as this is often a requirement. Health precautions are also crucial when visiting Guinea. It is recommended to consult with a healthcare provider about vaccinations and medications, particularly for diseases such as malaria. Carrying a basic first-aid kit can be useful for minor injuries or ailments during your travels. Staying hydrated and practicing good hygiene will also contribute to a healthy trip. In case of emergencies, having a list of local contacts, including your embassy and local hospitals, can be invaluable. Familiarize yourself with the local emergency numbers and keep important documents, such as your passport and insurance information, easily accessible. With these practical tips in hand, travelers can focus on enjoying all that Guinea has to offer. Indulge in the Flavors of Guinea The culinary scene in Guinea is a delightful fusion of flavors that reflects the country’s diverse cultures. Traditional dishes often feature staple ingredients such as rice, cassava, and yams, paired with a variety of meats and vegetables. One must-try dish is foutou, a delicious blend of mashed plantains or yams served with spicy sauces. This dish is a favorite among locals and is commonly enjoyed during family gatherings and celebrations. Another popular dish is jollof rice, a flavorful rice dish cooked with tomatoes, onions, and spices, often served alongside grilled chicken or fish. Each region puts its unique spin on this beloved dish, making it a culinary adventure to sample different variations. Street food is also a vibrant part of Guinea’s food culture, with vendors offering snacks such as fried plantains, grilled meat skewers, and spicy sauces that tantalize the taste buds. For those looking to experience the local dining scene, restaurants like Le Petit Bateau and Le Jardin de la Paix in Conakry offer a range of traditional dishes and international cuisine. These establishments provide a cozy atmosphere where visitors can enjoy a meal while soaking in the local ambiance. Exploring the flavors of Guinea is sure to be a highlight of any trip, offering a delicious way to connect with the culture. Experience the Vibrant Nightlife of Guinea As the sun sets, Guinea comes alive with a variety of nightlife options that cater to different tastes. In Conakry, the capital, visitors can find lively bars and clubs that showcase the local music scene. Venues like La Bodega and Le Club feature live performances from local artists, offering a fantastic opportunity to experience the rhythms of Guinea. Whether it’s traditional music or contemporary beats, the nightlife scene provides an energetic atmosphere for dancing and socializing. For a more laid-back evening, consider visiting a local café or lounge where you can enjoy a refreshing drink while mingling with locals. Many cafés offer outdoor seating, allowing guests to relax under the stars while savoring the cool evening breeze. Engaging in conversations with locals can provide insights into the culture and community, making for a memorable night out. Additionally, cultural performances and events often take place in the evenings, showcasing traditional dance and music. Attending these events not only provides entertainment but also offers a deeper appreciation of Guinea's artistic heritage. With a range of nightlife options available, visitors can find the perfect way to unwind after a day of exploration. Eco-Friendly Hotels for the Conscious Traveler For travelers who prioritize sustainability, Guinea offers a selection of eco-friendly hotels that minimize their environmental impact while providing comfortable accommodations. These hotels often incorporate green practices such as energy-efficient lighting, water conservation measures, and locally sourced materials. Staying at an eco-friendly hotel allows visitors to enjoy their stay while supporting responsible tourism initiatives. One notable option is the Hotel du Golfe de Guinee, which emphasizes sustainability in its operations. Guests can enjoy modern amenities while knowing they are contributing to environmentally friendly practices. The hotel’s commitment to sustainability extends to its restaurant, which features organic and locally sourced ingredients in its dishes. Choosing eco-friendly accommodations not only enhances the travel experience but also supports local communities and conservation efforts. By staying in hotels that prioritize sustainability, travelers can enjoy the beauty of Guinea while helping to preserve its natural resources for future generations. Unique Guinea Vacation Rentals for a Home Away From Home For those seeking a more personalized experience, vacation rentals in Guinea provide an excellent alternative to traditional hotels. These rentals range from cozy apartments in Conakry to charming houses in the countryside, offering travelers the chance to immerse themselves in local life. Many vacation rentals come equipped with kitchens, allowing guests to prepare their own meals and enjoy the flavors of Guinea at their leisure. Staying in a vacation rental also provides a unique opportunity to connect with local communities. Many hosts are eager to share their knowledge of the area, offering tips on attractions, dining, and cultural experiences. This personal touch can enhance the travel experience, making it feel more like a home away from home. With platforms available for booking vacation rentals, travelers can easily find accommodations that suit their needs and preferences. Whether seeking a beachfront property or a quiet retreat in the hills, Guinea’s vacation rentals offer a diverse range of options for every type of traveler.

Futuristic chrome was everywhere in the late ’90s and early ’00s, from Busta Rhymes and Janet Jackson’s “What’s It Gonna Be” music video to Britney Spears’s Teen People photo shoot. Maybe it was the excitement of a new millennium approaching. Perhaps it was all the nifty tech that was coming out. (Did we mention that Britney was posing with a robotic dog?) And our nails weren't spared from the shiny, silvery trend. My obsession with chrome nails started when I saw the poker scene in The Parent Trap. It was Annie (Lindsay Lohan) vs. Hallie (also Lohan), and the latter had blue lacquer with a chrome finish painted on her nails. To quote another Lohan masterpiece (Mean Girls), I was a woman possessed. I spent about 80% of my time trying to hunt down that exact Hard Candy polish in my small hometown of Greenville, Mississippi, and the other 20% of the time I was praying for someone to bottle the exact effect held by the foil on the back of the Dentyne Ice gum packet. Get the Daily Beauty Blast Newsletter The latest beauty treatments, trends, and hot takes in your inbox daily, as well as bonus curated beauty shopping guides. Brands like Sally Hansen launched Chrome Nail Makeup lacquers—which had that true molten metal finish—reserved for salon techniques and red-carpet events. “Chrome nails have a very editorial feel, like liquid metal on your fingertips,” says Jin Soon Choi, manicurist and founder of JinSoon. “This look is all about that ultra-reflective, mirror-like finish that feels futuristic but still chic, and it works beautifully for anyone.” By 2011, Minx Nail Wraps, worn by Beyoncé in her 2013 “Drunk In Love” video, scratched that chrome itch I couldn’t shake. But left to my own devices—a hair dryer, nail file, and an orange wood stick—the result was rarely as polished as when I’d have a set applied by a pro. Over a decade later, chrome nails are so back, and my nostalgic heart is singing to the tune of the Y2K playlist I keep on repeat. While Hailey Bieber may have provided the gateway into molten metal territory by popularizing glazed donut nails in 2022, celebrities like Addison Rae and Lisa have been working chrome accents, and DIY methods continue to flood #NailTok. “Back then, you had the classic silver chrome or gold, but now there are a variety of chrome powder shades to choose from,” says Los Angeles-based nail artist Sarah Chue. "And it was only applied to the entire surface of the nail. Now, chrome powder is used at the tips of your nails for a shiny French, on top of 3D art, and as an ombré.” Aja Walton is a Brooklyn-based manicurist and Essie ambassador. Today’s chrome nails are also much easier to maintain than those in 2000. “When chrome first came out, it was a challenge for some nail techs to prevent it from chipping,” says Galinda Jimenez, manicurist and OPI global education manager. “Because of this, a lot of techs shied away from using it. It was frustrating to have unsatisfied clients come back with chipped chrome.” But thanks to improvements in polish and topcoat formulas, it’s easier than ever to jump onto the trend. We’ve come a long way, baby. What colors should you choose for your chrome manicure? Chrome is the nail equivalent of a plain white tee: It’s flattering when paired with most colors. “Even something like butter yellow or bright orange can look unexpected and fun,” says Sojin Oh, a 3D nail artist based in Los Angeles. “Chrome just has a way of transforming color into something dimensional and striking.” Erica De Los Santos, nail artist and founder of Nail’d It Beauty Lounge, notes that a black base color is ideal if you want a true, mirror chrome effect, as the deep hue really makes the effect stand out. However, don’t knock those summer pastels or beachy neons just yet. Your manicurist likely has a chrome powder on hand to elevate any shade on the ROYGBIV spectrum. “One of the best things about chrome is how versatile it is,” De Los Santos says. “Whether you’re into bright neons, soft pastels, deep jewel tones, or classic nudes, chrome adds a unique reflective layer that enhances the base color and gives your manicure a playful, high-fashion twist.” Before heading to a salon, determine the look you’re after and make sure to bring inspiration photos if you have any. “You can ask to see the chrome powder options your salon offers, which can vary in color and finish,” says De Los Santos. “Be sure to communicate the exact effect you’re looking for—whether you prefer a bold, high-shine metallic look or a softer, pearlescent shimmer—so your technician can guide you to the right powder and base combination.” The only color combination we’d avoid is pairing white polish with a powder that has a white and silver shift. That’s what senior beauty editor Jesa Marie Calaor chose for her first attempt at glazed donut nails. She called me to say that her manicure looked like a set of bad veneers. I saw them, and they sure did. How to get chrome nails at home While a gel manicure gives chrome some extra staying power, you can totally achieve the look using traditional lacquers and chrome powder. “There is a small window of opportunity about two to three minutes after the second coat of your base shade is applied,” Jimenez says. “Rub the chrome onto the lacquer while it is dry enough to grip the powder. If it is too dry, you won’t be able to rub it in, but if it’s not dry enough, you can cause smudges.” One thing to consider: Chromes aren’t exactly forgiving when it comes to any ridges or uneven texture on your nails, so make sure to clean up your cuticles, buff out your nail bed, and pay extra attention to your nail shape when trying your hand at the technique. “Chrome tends to highlight every bump or imperfection in the nail,” says Mazz Hanna, manicurist and CEO of Nailing Hollywood. “If you're doing it yourself, make sure the nail surface is buffed and your base coat is even.” For the most seamless look, Aja Walton, Brooklyn-based manicurist and Essie ambassador, recommends filing your nails right after you’ve applied the powder, but before you seal it with a topcoat. Need some inspiration? We’ve got you. Credit: Brie Williams A good night's sleep is the best way to start the day, but have you ever wondered which is better—sleeping with the window open or closed? Similar to the topic of a room's brightness level during the night, people tend to have pretty strong opinions about whether you should crack the window open before bed or keep it shut. To find out whether there is a definitive answer, we asked sleep experts to weigh in on the subject. While it turns out that there is no objective research data to support either option and it may come down to your personal preference, experts say sleeping with the window open comes with benefits and disadvantages. As you'll see below, deciding whether to sleep with the window open or closed should come down to factors such as air quality, outdoor temperature, noise and brightness levels, and safety. Just as choosing the right bedroom paint color can play a role in your sleep quality, an open or closed window can affect how well you sleep at night. Jade Wu, PhD, DBSM, is a behavioral sleep psychologist and Mattress Firm's sleep health advisor. Should You Sleep with the Window Open or Closed? "Although there are as many potential pros as cons, we don’t have objective research data verifying these claims and there is data from polls showing a personal preference for one versus the other," says Safia S. Khan, M.D., a sleep disorders expert. While she notes that more than half of adults in the United States say they sleep with their windows closed, she explains that these numbers are significantly higher in densely populated cities and during the warmer months. Sleep experts say cracking the window open while you sleep offers several benefits. "Sleeping with the window open can improve air quality through better ventilation and lower CO₂, potentially enhancing sleep quality," says Raphael Vallat, PhD, lead machine learning data scientist at ŌURA. If the temperature outside is comfortable and there are no major allergens, Jade Wu, a behavioral sleep psychologist and Mattress Firm's sleep health advisor, says she generally prefers to leave the windows open for better fresh air circulation. "There is research showing that better air quality is associated with better sleep, however this is only true if the neighborhood air quality is good and there is not too much noise and air pollution," Wu says. While you may enjoy the fresh breeze coming in through the open window, sleep experts point out a couple of negative factors to be aware of. “The disadvantages are allergens and air pollutants, environmental noise, and bugs that can find their way into your space despite protective screens or netting," Khan says. Although increased air circulation can be one of the benefits of keeping the window open during the night, this can become a disadvantage in an area with poor air quality. "If it's too loud or bright outside, the fresh air may not be worth it," Wu says. Whether it's city noise or the sound of animals in a rural area, increased exposure to noise may be a big reason for to shut the windows at night. "Disturbance from morning noise can also be a deterrent to sleeping with the windows open,” Khan adds. Additionally, bright lights coming in through the window can pose another disturbance to a good night's sleep. If you sleep best when the room is completely dark, you'll want to keep the windows closed at night. Even if you have light-blocking blinds or blackout curtains, cracking a window open can affect the window treatments' effectiveness in eliminating light from the room. Lastly, sleeping with the window open can also pose a potential security risk depending on the area and ease of access to the bedroom windows. Keep all these factors, both positive and negative, in mind when choosing whether to sleep with the window open or closed.
